title: [PDF] How Descartes' Methodical Skepticism Explains Cognitive Biases ...
excerpts:
This challenge is not new. More than three centuries ago, René Descartes proposed a
method of systematic doubt to filter unreliable beliefs from those that could be known with
certainty. His methodical skepticism, detailed in *Meditations on First Philosophy* (1641), looked
to eliminate erroneous assumptions by doubting everything that could possibly be false, but
beyond its philosophical implications, Descartes’ skepticism can be understood as an early
model of cognitive regulation, one that is increasingly relevant to contemporary debates about
critical thinking, cognitive biases, and misinformation.
This paper reinterprets Descartes’ skepticism as a cognitive strategy, placing it in direct
dialogue with modern research on misinformation susceptibility, epistemic vigilance, and
metacognition. How do cognitive biases such as the Dunning-Kruger effect and confirmation
bias interfere with Descartes’ ideal of ration doubt? Does skepticism protect us from deception,

title: “I think, therefore I am”: René Descartes on the Foundations of Knowledge - 1000-Word Philosophy: An Introductory Anthology
excerpts:
Section Title: “I think, therefore I am”: René Descartes on the Foundations of Knowledge > ... > Notes
Content:
[<sup>[8]</sup>]() What follows from the certainty of the Cogito is the nature of Descartes himself: he must be a thing that thinks. The Cogito does not prove that Descartes has a body or a brain, or even that other minds exist: these can all be doubted. Only thought is certain: Descartes says, “ *I am, I exist* , that is certain. But for how long? For as long as I think. It might perhaps even happen that if I stopped thinking, I should at once altogether stop being.” Cottingham, John, Robert Stoothoff, and Dugald Murdoch. *The Philosophical Writings of Descartes: Volume 2.* Cambridge University Press (2012) (AT VII 27: CSM II 18).
[<sup>[9]</sup>]() Cottingham, John, Robert Stoothoff, and Dugald Murdoch. *The Philosophical Writings of Descartes: Volume 2.* Cambridge University Press (2012) (AT 7, 145-6, CMS 2, 104).

title: Series - The Age of Descartes
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Publications of the Center of Studies on Descartes and the Seventeenth Century
Content:
"The Age of Descartes" is a book series devoted to Descartes, Cartesianism, and seventeenth-century philosophy, which aims to cover this crucial stage in the History of Philosophy as fully as possible. In order to do so, the series seeks (a) also to include studies on scientific topics (including mathematical ones), rather than focusing exclusively on metaphysical and epistemological themes, and (b) to approach Cartesian and seventeenth-century philosophy both from the point of view of the History of Philosophy and from a more technical philosophical perspective, without prioritising either of both perspectives. The main languages of the series are English and French.
